Mr. President, within the EU there are various instances of languages belonging to historic European peoples that, because they are not associated with a state, are not permitted to be used in the debates of this Parliament. And this constitutes a democratic and representational deficit.

One such case is that of the Basque language, Euskera, which is not a minority language but an official language, at least in the southern part of the Basque Country, which administratively falls within the Spanish State.

But this is not the case, and please do not take it personally, Commissioner, with the northern Basque Country, which is part of the French State, whose president, before the United Nations Assembly, stated that denying respect to national identities and languages fosters humiliation, and that without such respect, there will be no peace in the world.
And then, neither Basque, nor Breton, nor Occitan receive the slightest official recognition nor do they have support to respect or promote their use.

Therefore, I urge the European Fundamental Rights Agency to ensure and work toward preventing the violation of the native language rights of any European people within the Member States, and to ensure that their inhabitants are not discriminated against, and that all such languages be recognized as official within their respective territories.
The thing is, rich folks, Jaume Andreoc, the wealthy gaitic, Europe with its modest pleasures, the village with its little joys.

Thank you, Madam President. First of all, I would like to thank our rapporteur, Claudio Fava, for his outstanding work in achieving a common position with the Council, which includes numerous improvements proposed by our Parliament.
With this directive, we once again demonstrate our commitment to building a common migration policy based on a comprehensive approach.
The approach of this directive is clear: to combat mafia networks, punish employers without scruples, and protect exploited immigrants who have no form of social protection.
We want the unjust low wages for immigrants to disappear, as they also distort average wages, particularly in sectors such as construction, agriculture, domestic work, and hospitality.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are numerous vested interests and significant amounts of money involved in the underground economy.
It is precisely during difficult times that it is more important than ever to manage migration flows intelligently, with generosity but also with responsibility.
The easy thing would be to succumb to the temptation of not attempting to address the underground economy.
We cannot turn a blind eye and leave around eight million undocumented immigrants in a state of vulnerability, facing working conditions approaching slavery.

Mr. President, if we wish for this directive to be effective, rigorous inspections are necessary, along with economic penalties and even criminal sanctions in the most aggravated cases, in order to have a deterrent effect on business operators.
We will thus succeed in reducing the informal economy and eliminating the migration incentive created by the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be clear that the only option for working in Europe is through legal channels.

Therefore, we wish to continue with the comprehensive approach and urge the Commission, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Baró, to present the new blue cards—quotation marks used for emphasis—as soon as possible for the other occupational categories.
Dear colleagues, Ms. Ferrero-Waldner, Mr. Solana, I would first like to thank the three rapporteurs for their work, and as the shadow rapporteur on the report on the EPC, I will refer to
specifically to this report, beginning by congratulating Mr. Sarisbolski on the outcome of his work and on the cooperation he has provided with the other groups to achieve a consensual result.

Just as this Parliament has repeatedly called for the European Union to be equipped with the necessary instruments to speak with one voice in the world, the Chamber itself also knows how to remain cohesive in assessing and advancing the major priorities in foreign and security policy.

Our group's effort, the Socialist Group, has followed a dual direction. On one hand, introducing or reinforcing major issues that are crucial for us, applied transversally across all foreign policy, such as the fight against climate change, the promotion of

peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, propose elements for rebalancing the geographical priorities of the Common Foreign and Security Policy compared to how they originally appeared in the text, if at all, or introduce them as new elements if they were absent.

In this way, we have advocated, for example, for greater clarity regarding the relationships between the institutional frameworks and cooperation mechanisms involved in the recently defined Eastern dimension.

We have advocated for greater diversification and greater significance in our relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when particularly virulent wars occur, and sometimes not even then.

With regard to Latin America, we have wished to reflect the current processes of negotiation of association agreements—the first bioregional agreements concluded by the European Union in its entire history.

Regarding the Mediterranean, we have opposed a reductionist approach that speaks solely of security, opting instead to include the full range of political, economic, and socio-cultural elements contained within the Barcelona process.

Regarding the amendments for the plenary session, our group has not submitted any, believing that doing so would in some way strengthen the balance achieved through the compromises, and therefore we will oppose most of the amendments in order not to weaken the agreement reached in the Committee on Foreign Affairs. Thank you.
Thank you, President. Venezuela's referendum has concluded with a victory for the "Yes" vote, gracefully acknowledged by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there has been no equality of opportunity in the campaign, and that the ruling party has had the overwhelming support of the entire state apparatus, while the opposition has suffered constant harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has remained practically divided into two halves, and it will be very difficult to build the future based solely on one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion, and consensus among Venezuelan political and social actors for the country's benefit.
It is not through visceral attitudes, dismissals, or insults that we will help Venezuela find its way toward a democratic, pluralistic, and free society.

In this regard, we criticize the Venezuelan Government's decision to expel a Spanish eurodeputy, and even more so the manner in which this expulsion was carried out. But we appeal to this Chamber to avoid...

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that violate local legislation in force, and even less so that they insult a head of state, no matter how deserving of criticism he may be.
With these attitudes, we are jeopardizing future missions of the European Parliament to other countries.

Mr. President, I have just returned from Venezuela, where I was invited by its electoral authority to serve as a member of an international electoral observation group for the referendum held on the 15th.

The European members of that group have submitted a report to the National Electoral Council, offering a generally positive assessment of the process in terms of its organization, transparency, participation, free and secret exercise of the right to vote, and security throughout all its stages.

Regarding the statements made by Deputy Herrero to a Venezuelan television channel, he pointed out that "they aimed to undermine the ongoing electoral process, level serious accusations against the democratic institutions of that country, and border on interference in the internal politics of a sovereign nation."
Our Parliament should not foster any kind of confrontation with the democratic institutions of Venezuela.
On the contrary, it would be up to the honorable member to provide explanations to this Chamber regarding an action that affects us all.

count those lines with a multiannual amount of allocated credit. There is credit allocated for 2009, but not beyond that.

emphasize the importance of the mid-term review of the financial framework, which should allow for adjustments to the maximum limits of the various headings.
Well, at this late hour on Monday, the Commission likely has more pressing matters than this report.
In short, what we are doing is simply responding to a report from the Commission, a report on the implementation of Regulation 1206/2001 of the
Council Directive of 28 May 2001 on the cooperation between the judicial authorities of the Member States in the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters.
We are in a sphere that is truly not community-based, but intergovernmental, in which the Council, through the regulation, has sought to encourage cooperation among those judicial bodies.
But by doing so, I believe there is a certain lack of rigor on the part of the European Union,

...that is, certain measures have been foreseen... for example, the issuance of practical guidelines... the use of electronic media... and so on, but I acknowledge that it is quite difficult... for the Commission to take action...

I do not know what the Commission has achieved or intends to achieve, but it seems quite difficult to accomplish, given the considerable challenges posed by this form of intergovernmental cooperation.
In other words, we are asking cooperating judicial authorities—27 countries in total—managing 22 different languages, with very different legal systems, to achieve results.
The Commission has noted that the results are not good, that there is significant delay in these cooperation mechanisms, and certainly that we lack the resources to do so.
The ideal would be to have material resources,
For example, what could the Commission do to equip judicial bodies, particularly those at a lower level, with technological resources such as a videoconferencing system?
Videoconferencing may be a utopia for many small courts across the European Union, but it appears to be the only way to achieve it.
I believe the European Union should rely on existing institutions, specifically the European Judicial Network,
We would need to strengthen this institution so that judges themselves, through their cooperation systems, could carry out this type of communication.
On the other hand, any training for judges is essential.
This is something the Commission cannot do on its own either.
but the Commission can encourage the development of this type of training courses in community law and in the knowledge of the various legal systems.
Then there is a major difficulty, namely the problem of languages, that is, how a Spanish judge can communicate with a Finnish judge when they speak such different languages.
One cannot expect judges, in addition to having to manage their own legal system—which is already complex enough—to be required to possess knowledge of such matters.
What can we do from a practical standpoint to assist judges in the task of interpretation and translation?
What could we do to help them do it?
in presenting this report, express no kind of rigor or severity toward the Commission.
I believe the work is quite difficult.
What I would like to know, taking advantage of the fact that we have here a commissioner who is interested in this issue, is what the Commission thinks can be done to provide a greater response?
rigor in this 2001 regulation, somewhat imprecise, somewhat arbitrary, and I do not know whether, from a practical standpoint, it may have any consequences or whether progress can be made with it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, I am very pleased to be able to conclude this legislative term with the final adoption of the last of the maritime packages.

However, I recall that when I arrived at the European Parliament in 1999, we experienced a major maritime disaster—the sinking of the vessel Erika—off the French coast, which gave rise to an initial set of legislative proposals aimed at preventing and addressing such accidents.
We thought we had already overcome the danger, but shortly afterward, the coasts of Galicia were devastated by oil from another, even greater accident than the first—the personal vessel.

Since then, in the Transport Committee, we have worked on successive legislative packages, striving to overcome the numerous resistances we encountered from shipowners, oil companies, classification societies, and also certain Member States that rejected our proposals, considering them overly demanding.

Over these ten years, we have adopted various directives and regulations, some of which we have had to revise again because, as a result of cuts imposed by the Council, they proved ineffective in their initial formulation.

We have adopted the rules for the introduction of double-hull vessels, the European Fund for Major Oil Spills, and safe harbors. And now we are reviewing and adopting the liability of maritime passenger transporters and the investigation of incidents.

maritime accidents, the Community monitoring and information system for maritime traffic, control by the port State, and finally the regulation and directive concerning organizations for ship inspection and classification.
And all of this is good and desirable, and should enter into force and be applied by all member states as soon as possible.
For this reason, I feel satisfied.
But the legislative term comes to an end, I leave Parliament, and I go with the regret of not having succeeded in making the implementation of existing control systems mandatory.
They are patented to determine when and how much of anchor and fuel tank waste has been illegally discharged into the sea by a specific vessel.
That is, a kind of black boxes or tachometers that can be inspected by maritime authorities each time a vessel arrives at port.

I believe we owe the marine environment a greater level of attention and stricter procedures to address the extensive pollution we generate, as failing to do so will impact our food chain and our lives, ultimately making us pay a heavy price.
I trust that they will not have to wait another ten years to more effectively and efficiently control illegal marine discharges.
Mr. President, the review of the Noviñeta Directive is a matter of great importance that concerns us all, particularly peripheral countries, which feel themselves to be victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to address the internationalization of the external costs of road transport by attributing three new costs: atmospheric pollution, noise pollution, and congestion.

Although the principles underlying the proposal—those who use pay and those who pollute pay—are reasonable, the solution proposed is by no means reasonable, as it will be discriminatory toward a sector that has been suffering for months from the disastrous effects of the economic crisis.
Demonizing road freight transport, although not the intention, has turned out to be the outcome.

Madam President, this proposal is ill-timed, and far from achieving the expected goals of sustainable transport, it would be a death sentence for a large number of European small and medium-sized enterprises that employ thousands of people.

people in the European Union who daily contribute to the distribution of goods, ensuring products reach their final consumers. A consumer who will see how their products become more expensive due to the implementation of these fees.
Ladies and gentlemen, we need a competitive, sustainable, and environmentally friendly transportation system, without overlooking the road, as it is currently the only means of reaching everywhere.
Modal change remains a dream, far from being a reality.
Trans-European networks, sea lanes, cross-border connections remain, in some cases, projects rather than reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, when the titanic of the global economy sinks, it is not possible to ask the orchestra to continue playing, let alone for joy.

On July 15, 2008, the European Commission adopted a communication addressed to the Council and the Parliament, recommending the establishment of a strategic partnership between the European Union and Mexico.
In turn, the European Council on Foreign Relations recognized Mexico as a strategic partner on October 13, pending the Parliament's opinion on the matter.
Let us recall that already at the seventh meeting of the Joint Parliamentary Committee EU-Mexico, held at the end of October last year, we stated that a
The strategic partnership between Mexico and the European Union represents a necessary impetus to strengthen and develop the true potential of our bilateral relations.
We highlighted the favorable state of relations between both parties and the positive assessment of the current comprehensive agreement.

We emphasize the need for our respective executives to strengthen cooperation in political, economic, and developmental matters, particularly in the face of the new challenges of all kinds arising from the financial and economic crisis shaking our continents.

With the great country that Mexico is, we share values and objectives such as the development of democratic forms of government, commitment to gender equity, consolidation of the rule of law, equitable and sustainable development, and respect for human rights.
We advocate strengthening cooperation in the fight against organized crime, terrorism, and drug trafficking based on the principles of shared responsibility and strict adherence to international law.
Consequently, we will support the proposal for recommendations on the European Union-Mexico Strategic Association, which will be submitted to Parliament this morning.
With regard to the report on microcredit, I would like to congratulate all the rapporteurs, as they have done outstanding work.
undoubtedly improved the Commission's initiative
And I understand that the recommendations established here will enable us to lay the foundation for developing an authentic framework for the microcredit sector in the European Union.
It has been successful in many developing countries, even in some European ones, both as a mechanism for economic activity and as a tool to enhance social inclusion and job creation.

but that had not yet been transferred to the sector or the community context. And I believe this presents an opportunity to do so, particularly given the economic and financial context in which we find ourselves.
prioritized in this regard, strengthen the initiatives underway here, but further steps must be taken.

the European guarantee for microcredit loans as an instrument that can facilitate such access and should be included in the report.
And I conclude by thinking that this will undoubtedly lay the foundations for us to create a harmonious framework within the European context that will drive forward the microcredit sector.

Madam President, taking advantage of the fact that we are discussing the budget, I understand that we must prepare ourselves mentally for next year’s negotiation regarding the continuation of coal subsidies, an essential indigenous energy source.
And I say this with due advance notice, because an inappropriate senior official, confusing public opinion, felt free to express an opposing view, leaking it to an economic publication.
None of that
Support for coal in my country will have to continue after 2012, and I wish this to be recorded accordingly in the minutes of the session.
Mr. Officials, confine yourselves to implementing the current plan, and thereafter, starting in 2012, to the instrument we begin negotiating with the sector next year.
Thank you, Mr. President. Commissioner, this question, as Mr. Bocland has explained, is particularly timely, given the diplomatic conference on the ship recycling convention that will be held in May.
Therefore, I would like to congratulate Mr. Bloch, not only for the question but also for the resolution he has prepared.

In the People's Party we support the fight against the improper practices and methods being used to reduce ship sizes, and therefore we back Mr. Brokland's resolution proposal. However, there are certain points that concern us.

The first is paragraph 3, which he has also already pointed out, stating that ships in their entirety must be considered hazardous waste and, as a consequence, must fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have submitted an amendment to this section because there are differing interpretations both within and outside the European Union, and we believe the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship recycling that defines the specifics of its implementation.
The draft resolution also requests in point 14 a financing mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

We find it rather premature at this stage to speak of mandatory contributions, given that the International Maritime Organization has not yet decided which system will be adopted for this financing. Moreover, I believe the European Commission also lacks a clear position on this matter.
Finally, in point 15, the commission is urged to clearly establish that the State with jurisdiction over waste is the responsible State.
We believe it is the Commission's responsibility to decide this matter, which should also be settled through an international agreement.

The key is to ensure coherence between the future ship recycling agreement and the Basel Convention on the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal, taking into account not only the Basel Convention but also all existing legislation.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, with whom we share substantial agreement regarding this program, and to congratulate ourselves all, as it is essential during times of economic crisis to maintain a program of this nature, one that delivers clear positive social impacts.

Although we are facing a consultation procedure from the European Parliament, we must send a clear political signal that, at this time of economic uncertainty emanating from Brussels and Strasbourg, we are not exclusively concerned or

not only concerned with the recovery of the financial system, but we are fully aware that social policies, particularly those supporting those who need it most, the poorest in the European Union, must also grow.

This food distribution program must continue to be regarded as part of the Common Agricultural Policy. Here we agree with the Commission. Not only because European agriculture has a distinctly social character, but also because it is an effective instrument, because it is more needed than ever at present, and because it must continue to function.

From the Socialist Group, just like the rapporteur, we oppose the co-financing of this programme between the European Union and the Member States, as this would result in discrimination among States according to their wealth, potentially leading to situations where the programme cannot be implemented in the countries most in need.

It is not understood why, precisely at a time when it is most needed, the Commission seeks to save Community funds in the most social aspect of the CAP, especially considering that there are budgetary surpluses every year in the agricultural budget.
Full community funding is essential for us.
We need to ensure that this program reaches all countries, and above all, the most vulnerable ones.
43 million potential beneficiaries of this program are demanding a greater effort from us, and in no case should budgetary spending be cut.
Austerity yes, but not at the expense of the poorest.
And it is precisely to ensure that it reaches citizens in all member states that the program must cover the entire journey of the distributed food.

Therefore, on behalf of the Socialist Group, for which I am responsible for the report, we have submitted an amendment to ensure that all expenses related to distribution— including transportation, storage, and administrative costs—are financed through community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that food should preferably be community-based and of high quality.
It is the position consistent with that held by this Parliament in other similar programs recently discussed in this Chamber, such as the school fruit distribution plan.
Nothing more, and thank you very much.
We hope that the Commission will take into account this Parliament's position when the vote takes place, and above all that the plan will be implemented in the European Union as soon as possible.
The Arctic region is one of the most fragile regions on our planet.
The consequences of unrestricted exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic not only for the immediate environment and indigenous population, but for the world as a whole.

The melting of extensive areas has brought these risks to the forefront, making it necessary to establish a new global regulation to protect the Arctic, similar to the existing framework for the Antarctic, while taking into account the differences already highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty among all involved parties, undoubtedly including the European Union, is necessary to protect its unique environment, ensure the full sustainability of all types of human activities, and multilaterally regulate navigation through the new maritime routes becoming accessible.
The Arctic Council, during its many years of existence, has served as a model of cooperation for addressing common challenges.

At this time of difficulty and uncertainty, we must elevate that spirit and harmony to higher levels, in order to prevent the states of the region or other international actors from becoming embroiled in geostrategic rivalries, thus forgetting what must remain our common goal: the preservation of a great shared heritage.
Mr. President, unlike the two speakers who have just preceded me, I agree with the majority of those who have spoken.
This is a good directive, an excellent directive, aimed at protecting interpreters.
When discussing the need to adopt a smarter model for doing business, what is actually being talked about is doing away with intellectual property.
because business can only be conducted if there is a settled right.

achieved within the Committee on Legal Affairs thanks to the work of the rapporteur, Mr. Crowley, and the various shadow rapporteurs, such as Ms. McGill and Mr. Tubongo.
I believe it is a excellent directive, one that will enhance the creation of intellectual property.

I believe that the approval by this Chamber of that report, and I hope that the Council will align itself with the positions of the Council, the Commission, and the Parliament, will serve to ensure that in the next legislative term the

Let the Commission continue along this path, make proposals to Parliament, and advance in the field of intellectual property protection, which is essential for the development of our European Union as a major institution based on a shared culture.

Yes, Mr. President, the information you provide me is correct, but the impression we European parliamentarians, and ultimately European citizens, have is that we face a very dangerous gap in community law.
At this moment, as fiscal demands are increasingly reinforced in each of the member states, mobility is almost impossible.
And when we asked the Commission, the reason for the pragmatic approach is that the Commission never finds support in the Council.

We are caught in a vicious cycle whereby the Commission refers us to the Council and the Council refers us back to the Commission. Yet meanwhile, the reality is that citizens living in this Europe we wish to build cannot move between different countries or establish cross-border ties due to the heavy fiscal burden resulting from the lack of fiscal harmonization.
fiscal. Can the Council do something to get us out of this vicious cycle?
Madam President, I am pleased, for once, that the regular member is not here, that Mr. Tajani has come instead, because Mr. Tajani has the advantage of having been a European parliamentarian.
And as a European parliamentarian, I know you have experienced the frustration we European parliamentarians feel regarding the implementation of community law.

Now, in Parliament we tend to criticize the Commission. I believe we are asking the Commission to perform an impossible task, because all Community law and the enforcement of Community law are based on indirect application.

That is, the Commission has only a few officials at its headquarters, where some complaints arrive and where it has limited scope for action. But at this moment, there is also a trend toward restricting budgetary powers, meaning the Commission will no longer have the ability to act.
All community law, and the application of community law, is based on the actions of national authorities, national parliaments, national courts, and national officials.
And in this regard, I do not think we can expect too much from the Commission.
What we need to do is assist the Commission. And I believe that in Mrs. Frazzoni's report there are certain points that could be useful in achieving this implementation.
I am referring to those relating to the correlation of directives, the cooperation of national parliaments, and the actions of national courts.
Mr. President, two years ago, during a mission led by my colleague and fellow group member here present, Marian, I had the opportunity to visit.
and of being present in this court and of being aware of the immense work that this court was carrying out, not only for Sierra Leone but for all of humanity.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone, as has already been stated here, has undoubtedly set a precedent.

A precedent, as the resolution rightly points out, because it is the first international tribunal funded by voluntary contributions, the first court established in the country where the alleged crimes took place, and also, as already noted, the first tribunal to try a former prime minister.

For all these reasons, not only because it sets a precedent but also because it serves as a reference for other tribunals established ad hoc, such as those for Rwanda, the former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, or Lebanon, we consider it essential that this resolution be adopted, which we have been working on for several months in close collaboration with the tribunal.
Two years ago, we adopted a resolution to support its funding. Two years ago, the court was going through a difficult period during which it was running out of decisions.
...and it did not have sufficient support. Here, we must also thank the European Commission for stepping forward, so to speak, in financial support of this court.

We now ask above all for two things. First, that those convicted serve their sentences. What is at stake is no longer merely the functioning of the court, which ends next year, but the legacy that this court will leave behind. And second, obviously, all of this must be accompanied by increased funding.
In short, the Special Court of Sierra Leone is an example and a reference for all of us and for all courts that have judged war crimes.

It is an example and a reference, and it is a lesson that the second poorest country on this planet is teaching us, where upon entering the courthouse we saw a motto written: "Without justice, there is no peace."
And precisely for that reason, we have a moral obligation, not only as Europeans but as human beings, for the legacy of this court to remain permanent in history.

Yes, thank you, Mr. President. In the briefest possible terms, which Your Lordships will appreciate at this hour. Mr. Vice-President of the Committee, many thanks once again, as you have demonstrated, as you did in the third package and so on...

that its drive, its political standing, has been able to push forward an agreement at the Council and make it possible for the Parliament, the Council, and the Commission to reach agreement on this matter at this stage and in these final days of the legislative term.

I was just one minute short. Before that, I would like to respond to the lady Commissioner, who knows of my friendship and affection for her, and indeed of my friendship and affection for Mr. Orbán.
But the commissioner has not said a single word about the ombudsman's recommendation.
That is, the Ombudsman, who is fully aware of all the arguments provided by the Commissioner, has made a specific recommendation that the Committee appears to ignore in every respect.

I don't even want Mrs. Cuneva to reply to me, as it's obvious she's not the one who has the information. But all the data she has provided are consistent with what we already knew and with what the Ombudsman also knows. And she doesn't tell us whether in fact...

Despite all that, and since the ombudsman, who is aware of these issues, recommends changing the regulation, he does not tell us whether the Commission will adopt what the ombudsman requests and what the Parliament recommends based on the ombudsman's request.
Mr. President, I believe the regulatory proposals submitted to us by the Commission are important and necessary. On the other hand, it was also important and necessary that, from the
European Parliament, we insist on the principles emphasized by the two rapporteurs of the European Parliament, Mr. Zivieska and Mr. Depré, which is the principle of community competence.

This is a matter of community competence, in which, for practical reasons, it is convenient to maintain certain competences for the Member States, but, as Mr. Barró has stated, these must be limited in scope and duration.
Community competence is not available for renunciation, and neither the Council, nor the Commission, nor the Parliament has the authority to waive such community competences.

Having established this, it is an exceptional procedure. I believe the amendments we have discussed and submitted, which I think will be approved by Parliament tomorrow, will allow the adoption of this package of measures in first reading.

I also hope that, moving forward, the Commission will take this as a starting point and enable progress in developing a genuine European private law, which is becoming increasingly necessary, as highlighted, for example, by Mr. Sogor in his most recent intervention.

We are talking about the problems that most directly affect people, and to the extent that we are able to address these people's problems, the people will realize that the European Union has a role to play.
In short, Mr. President, I would like to congratulate my colleagues, Mr. Debrey and Mr. Esviesca, and thank the Commission and the Council for their willingness to cooperate with us on this issue.

Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, I have signed this resolution and requested to speak because I was a witness to what was called an expulsion, but which in reality was the kidnapping of our colleague Luis Herrero.

Moreover, I was able to confirm that, in Venezuela, political persecution, abuse of power by the Chavista regime, intimidation of opponents, threats, disregard for human dignity, and the arbitrary use of justice are common practices, among other things.
The case of Manuel Chávez is the final straw that has led to this resolution. Yet there are thousands of cases as egregious as his, cited in the resolution and others that are not.
as is the case with Ocedeño's son, who was born in the humble Caracas neighborhood of Petare, well known for its danger and also because he voted for a mayor who was not from the Chavista regime.

Eligio studied with the help of others, and specifically with support from Citibank, he managed to establish his own business, Banco de Caracas, and later lived normally while helping those in greatest need.
Today, however, he remains imprisoned in a Caracas prison, illegally, two years after being detained without any formal charges.
His only crime was being part of the economic oligarchy.
Another case is Niso Moreno, a student leader at the Universidad de los Andes, repeatedly elected to the University Council and president of the Federation of University Centers.
He won the 2003 elections for the Federation, the current Minister of the Interior and Justice. And this is his crime.
He is currently charged with attempted homicide, lascivious and violent acts, and the like, despite having been cleared of all previous charges.

Situations such as yours are part of everyday life in this country, where persecuting opponents with the intent of excluding them from political life and discouraging dissent have become commonplace occurrences.

However, from here it is necessary to send a message of hope to Venezuelan democracy despite the difficulties. I am confident that democracy will prevail and that President Chávez will be removed from office through the ballot box.

Madam President, Commissioner, indeed it is undeniable that progress has been made in recent times; but despite all the progress achieved, the South of the Union continues to burn, and burns more each day.
That is why the resolution that this Parliament is about to adopt cannot be just another resolution serving merely to highlight our profound concern over the misfortunes of others.
Forest fires occur due to one cause or another, but no one can deny that they are fueled by climate change.

And regardless of any responsibilities that might exist in certain states or autonomous communities, the fact remains that this Parliament must clearly call on the Commission to act swiftly and to mobilize all available resources—resources that are by no means insignificant—to this end.
Neither the victims, nor the damages suffered, nor the needs for reforestation understand bureaucracy, nor can they comprehend budgetary shortfalls.

Priority must be established and action must be taken. And action, in this case, means adjusting strategies, mobilizing resources, increasing them, and optimally coordinating preventive policies, demanding, if necessary,

case responsibilities on states that lack them. But assuming this is not a problem limited to some southern European states, and that climate change does not affect only certain states, but rather all states, making it a shared concern.
Therefore, one of the most immediate tasks facing this Parliament must be to ensure that the European Union secures at the upcoming Copenhagen summit clear commitments and
strong measures to maximize the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, as they are clearly at the root of fires, their causes, and these in turn are causes of further climate change.
Therefore, adopting this resolution is undoubtedly the challenge facing this Parliament now.
But that challenge set by the Commission must be met, if you truly, Mr. Commissioner, wish to prevent the south of the Union, which certainly exists, from continuing to burn.
Mr. President, thank you very much for granting me the floor. I address the Commissioner Barroso.

Mr. Barroso, I must tell you that I have followed you with great interest and deep respect for a long time, ever since you were in power in a country that is very close to us and deeply cherished—Portugal.
I must tell you, however, that you now have me completely surprised.

I am surprised for two reasons. First, because you have shown disregard for the group I belong to—the group of those not enrolled—by not granting us even one minute of your time to come and present your political proposals.
We represent many European citizens, and you have shown contempt for all of our voters.
Second, due to the fragility of their political project, and the inadequacy of their political proposals in the document "Political Guidelines for the Next Commission."
Proposals that do not even manage to incorporate what you have defended throughout your own tenure as President of the Commission.
I am surprised in this regard that issues such as energy, which have been well addressed throughout your term, now, when you present your proposals for the next...

Madam Commissioner, you concluded your remarks by telling us there is a crisis in the dairy sector, yet you began by informing us that all measures have already been taken and that the situation is being resolved.
Look, correcting oneself is the mark of wisdom.
And that is a Spanish saying that could very well apply to the case we are addressing today.

The dairy sector has been suffering from one of the worst crises in its history for several months, and despite this, neither you, nor the European Commission, nor some of the governments—including, it seems, my own, the Spanish government—have been willing to backtrack on the decisions made during the health check of the CAP.
These decisions were made without taking into account the fragility of this sector and in a market context markedly different from the one that emerged after that commitment ended.
gives the impression that the member states either did not want or were unable to see the storm approaching at that time.
The reform became outdated shortly thereafter due to the dramatic shift that has occurred in the market, a shift that demonstrates how the sector remains entirely subject to price volatility.

That major producers such as France and Germany—countries that achieved the largest absolute increase in their national production quotas under that agreement—should now be the ones calling for a review of the inspection decisions is highly thought-provoking.

In my view, the Member States were mistaken and should have paid closer attention to the voices—including mine—that called for postponing any definitive decision on the future of the sector until 2011.
A pity that they did not listen to us.
Do it now.

The breakdown of constitutional order in Honduras breaks two decades of stability without coups in Latin America and sets a perverse precedent for other countries, particularly the most vulnerable, as is currently the case with Paraguay.
The European Parliament cannot and must not remain deaf and silent in the face of such a violation of the rule of law.

Both the Council and the Commission have unequivocally condemned the coup, supporting President Arias's mediation in line with the position adopted unanimously by the OAS, backed by the United Nations.

The self-proclaimed de facto president, Micheletti, has violated recently reached agreements, mocking the international community and, among other measures, preventing the return of the constitutionally designated president, Mr. Zelaya.

Under current circumstances, the election of a new president in the polls scheduled for November 29 suffers from an origin of illegitimacy, and its results cannot and should not be recognized by the international community.

The European Parliament would make a serious mistake by sending a delegation to observe the elections, as this would be interpreted as endorsing the coup d'état. The OAS has already ruled out sending an observation mission to these elections.
This Parliament is characterized by being a guarantor of freedom, democracy, the rule of law, and respect for human rights.
As Bacla Havel stated this morning, there cannot be two weights or two measures.
We are all democrats here, and therefore activists of freedom. And thus, whether we speak of Cuba, China, North Korea, or now Honduras, our stance toward freedom must be resolute.
Let us not be blinded by our ideological preferences.
There are no right-wing or left-wing coup leaders or dictators, only liberticides and oppressors.

Many of us fear that conditions in Honduras will not change, and thus a most dangerous precedent will have been established, with the doctrine of the "good, temporary, and not overly dramatic coup" having triumphed.

I would like to congratulate the members of the Committee on Petitions, and its chairman, Mr. Leine, for the work they have done in enabling us to have a resolution such as the one we now possess and which we must approve unequivocally.
We can settle for merely discussing having enough through sectoral agreements, because speaking of a patchwork agreement already acknowledges a degree of failure.
We must go forward to approve this resolution, which has three noble aspirations.
A more evident one is avoiding climate change, with concrete proposals for emission reductions, committed funding, and aiming for a binding agreement—meaning, not a patchwork of partial measures.

And two underlying aspirations. One is to begin working earlier rather than later on establishing a common energy policy, just as we once created a common agricultural policy because it was necessary. And the other, of course, is to regain citizens' trust in politics and politicians, a resource in which we in Europe are certainly not abundant.
In this regard, the success of the summit will be the success of the three aspirations.
That is why I encourage the outgoing President of the Council to maintain his optimism, to press forward and negotiate as much as possible to draw others into this success.

I do not know whether the leaders of this multipolar world wish to be judged by history for failing to achieve a binding agreement. But as a Member of Parliament, and I believe all representatives of the peoples cannot be held accountable by history for failing to secure a binding commitment in Copenhagen, one that we owe to humanity to prevent climate change.

Today there is no doubt about the European Union's commitment to combating terrorism, and thanks to the Stockholm Treaty, the Stockholm Programme will enable us to continue advancing in this area of freedom, justice, and security that is so essential.

But in my view, this struggle has been based on mutual trust among the member states. Yet it is high time—especially given the opportunities offered by the Treaty of Lisbon—to harmonize national legislations in the direction of abolishing the statute of limitations for terrorist offenses, which should be regarded as crimes against humanity.

The day this happens, no one in this Parliament or in any other will defend terrorists, as has occurred, is occurring, and may continue to occur, causing further harm to the victims of terrorism, who see those who murdered their loved ones being treated as heroes, while they suffer in silence.
I therefore request that this Parliament promote or adopt the European Charter of Rights of Terrorism Victims, grounded in the defense of human memory.
Thank you, Madam President. I would first like to congratulate Mrs. McGuinness on this initiative, which highlights the important role played by the CAP in addressing international food supply needs.

Now that the European Commission appears intent on reducing the share of the CAP within the community budget, it must be stated clearly and loudly that the CAP is no longer a priority, but food sufficiency should be. In past decades, it has become evident that without the CAP, food sufficiency is very difficult, if not impossible, to achieve.
Agriculture cannot therefore be compared to other sectors of the economy capable of thriving in a free market, because the food market is not a free market.

The farmer requires the support of the European Union in order to sustain his business, and in turn, the Union needs the farmer to maintain an agricultural model capable of supplying sufficient, high-quality food to an increasingly demanding citizenry.
I therefore believe that the CAP would need to undergo a change in direction, but not disappear.

To achieve this, it would be necessary to ensure direct support for farmers, as well as to restore a policy for managing agricultural markets that provides greater price stability, benefiting not only farmers but also consumers and third countries.

It would be necessary to establish a framework of best practices to foster balanced relationships among the various actors in the food chain, prevent abusive practices, and promote a fairer distribution of commercial margins.
Moreover, a consumer information policy is needed that emphasizes the efforts made by community producers to comply with European Union environmental regulations.
environment, food safety, and animal welfare, as community producers must compete with imports from third countries where standards applied to those imports are significantly lower.

And those producers from third countries prefer exporting to the European Union rather than supplying the markets of their own countries, due to genuine profitability concerns, supported by the negotiations of the WTO.
According to a United Nations report, around 270,000 people were victims of human trafficking in the European Union in 2009.

With these figures in hand, it is essential that the European Union's action be primarily focused on the protection of victims, with particular attention to the situation of women and children, who are the most vulnerable.
We cannot continue to remain indifferent spectators to this theater, this spectacle of sexual exploitation unfolding before our eyes.
That is why I fully support this Parliament's request that assistance to victims be unconditional, more robust, and that penalties be stricter—something the honorable Barrot has just reminded us of.
Having said that, I would like to request the President and the Council to make full use of all European legislation within their power—existing and future—to protect victims of human trafficking.

I believe that the proposal for a European Order for the Protection of Victims, which I personally requested from the Spanish Presidency during the debates on the Stockholm Programme and which has ultimately come to light, will be an effective tool in combating these crimes.

And I expect a firm commitment, as we have already seen, from the Spanish Presidency to ensure that this directive provides victims with effective special protection measures throughout the entire territory of the European Union.

I hope that those of us who bear the responsibility of addressing society's most serious problems provide a real and concrete response to this grave issue of human trafficking, and that we do not rest content with mere good words.
We owe them truly to all the victims.

It has already been a long time since Fidel Castro and his oligarchy extinguished the ideals of the Cuban Revolution, which once resonated so powerfully and inspired such hope among peoples around the world, among the most outstanding youth of the international community.
What characterizes, however, the dictatorship of the Castro brothers at this moment is their impiety toward political and conscience prisoners, their impiety against their own people.
It reminds me of Franco's dying days, with the mistreatment of political prisoners that he likewise considered commonplace. His own obsessive disdain for exiles.
Castro ends up like Franco, and like all enemies of freedom when they entrench themselves in power.
But we must save lives and show solidarity with those who struggle and suffer.
It is our duty as Europeans committed to democracy, freedom, and human rights.
Thank you, Madam President. President of the Council, Commissioner, let us never again allow our silence to become complicity with those who destroy liberty.

Let us never again allow a person fighting for their rights and those of all others to die in a Cuban prison or anywhere else without our raising a strong and resolute voice demanding their salvation.

Mr. Orlando Zapata Tamayo, a 42-year-old Afro-descendant mason who merely demanded improved prison conditions, died after 86 days on hunger strike and seven years in prison for advocating respect for human rights.
and during those seven years he was mistreated, humiliated, and degraded by his jailers, amid, it must be remembered, the dense silence of the international community.
Other prisoners and human rights activists in Cuba are now on hunger strike, including psychologist and journalist Guillermo Fariñas.

This week, International Women's Day was celebrated.
In this Parliament, we had many posters in all the languages of the European Union stating that yes, violence against women can be ended.

Today we are discussing violence in Mexico, and it seems natural to acknowledge that Mexican women, both indigenous and non-indigenous, are the victims least protected by local and federal authorities.
Mexican women are victims of all forms of violence, from domestic violence to sexual violence and torture committed by members of the army or police, up to homicide.
Unfortunately, to date, the responses by Mexican authorities have been ineffective, which is why it is accurate to speak of near-total impunity.
Our resolution calls on the Mexican government to combat femicides—a harsh but accurate term—when we see that nearly all murders committed against women remain unpunished.
For example, in the State of Mexico, over the past four years, only 11% of the criminals who murdered nearly 700 women were convicted.
At the same time, it appears that women are the preferred victims of the judicial system.
There is no other way to explain how it was possible that two indigenous women, Alberta Alcántara and Teresa González, were sentenced to 21 years in prison and up to
They have now spent more than three years in prison for having kidnapped six men from the Federal Bureau of Investigation on their own during an incident at a market.
research. I hope the judge, who will rule on the appeal in this case within a week, recognizes the absurdity and injustice of the situation. But violence against women begins within the family.

I hope that Mexican legislators will soon improve the General Law on Women's Access to a Life Free from Violence, so that the types of violence are clearly defined and mechanisms are established to make the regulations operational.
Likewise, it would be customary to establish that domestic violence and spousal rape are criminal offenses.
Moreover, for Mexican society to change and reject violence against women, it is essential that this issue become a primary concern in the education of young people in schools.

not merely as witnesses to a traditional European culture, but as genuine agents generating wealth and employment—employment that we so desperately need at this time of economic crisis and dramatic employment situations in some Member States.

Mr. President, it has been necessary for us to face the brink and economic collapse before heads of state and government have understood that the path is not one of greater nationalism, but of greater Europe.
At this juncture, measures are being implemented, such as the decline of the veto right in the European Council or the seed of a European public treasury.

Mr. President, Commissioner, colleagues, this is a regulation of utmost importance for consumers and the food industry, as it is by no means easy to achieve balanced labeling that includes everything essential, yet is not so exhaustive as to complicate the information.

measure. Labels must include information on fats, sugars, and trans fats,

Aquaculture is an opportunity, as Mr. Martín Fragueiro has said, within the framework of the CAP, as the Commissioner herself stated.

A coherent framework for coordination must be provided, and the possibility of regularization must be granted, because aquaculture can provide self-sufficiency, sustainability, and economic resources, and I believe this is important for Europe.

an error in framing, Mr. President.

During these past six months of Spanish presidency, I would like to distinguish on one hand the political leadership of it, and on the other hand the work carried out by the officials who have been operating behind the scenes.
It is the first one that has failed, and this is due to the absence of proper leadership from the presidency of the government, which has instead been shaped more by pendulum swings.
However, I wish to highlight the efforts of the officials, their valuable commitment to serving Europe and Spain.
And the truth is, Mr. President, Spain has excellent professional civil servants, and what is regrettable is that, in daily reality, they are too often sidelined.
That said, it is appropriate to acknowledge that some commendable initiatives have been undertaken in these six months.
Among them, I am pleased to cite the organ donation and transplantation plan, and the treatment afforded to patients' rights in cross-border healthcare.
Even less settled in the air are the agreements regarding the new 2020 strategy, and above all those concerning the European External Action Service, both of which remain too fragile for now.
Regrettably, what has occurred with the European Protection Order for Victims of Gender Evidence has been lamentable, and the same bleak outcome has been offered by the
a basic European patent for the lives of thousands of businesses, partially blocked by linguistic issues stimulated by Spanish legislation.

Yet all major political actions have ended in spectacular failure. From the Mediterranean summit to the European Union summit with the United States, the latter being the one about which its own government was responsible for generating exaggerated expectations.
However, we must agree that being unable to implement their policy toward Cuba has been highly beneficial for the Cubans.
Finally, the progress achieved on the path toward better European economic governance cannot in any way be attributed to her presidency, conspicuously absent on the most relevant issues.
Goodbye, then, to the Spanish presidency.

A positive conclusion I can draw from this experience is that my fantasy as a committed Europeanist is excited by the thought that it might signal the end of rotating presidencies as they have been conceived until now.
This would be good news, for as long as Europe fails to strengthen its common institutions, firmly uniting its fragments, it will continue to risk being engulfed by the voids of irrelevance.
Mr. President, believe me, Mr. Zapatero, I would have preferred to deliver a different speech today.
Your Lordship set forth a semester filled with ambition and established such high expectations that, when compared to what was actually achieved, disappointment is inevitable.
The truth is that Your Lordship has been unable to overcome a complex situation that required leadership and credibility.
Your Lordship has at times recalled that the European Union is a club with rules, and I agree.
But it was his lordship who was responsible for enforcing compliance with those rules.
The reality is that it was to the Government of Spain that they urgently demanded the fulfillment of their obligations.
In this painting, positive achievements that have not been lacking have been diminished.
Your Lordship has had time to recount them; I have not.
Democracy, Your Honor, is a system governed by public opinion.
And that is indeed what it appears to be. Obama's disdain, the cancellation of the Mediterranean summit, and the announced EU-U.S. union represent failures of considerable magnitude.
The rush to close improvised agreements at any price has not enhanced the image of Spanish management. The method used to achieve some respectable objective has been described as clumsy.
Spain is a great nation and will remain so despite poor management during this period.
Our diplomats and officials have maintained their dignity, and thanks to this, Spain's prestige will endure.
The truth is that the evaluation of this presidency will weigh more on Spain's situation than on what I have or have not done here.
It would be difficult for someone who has placed their country in a period of uncertainty and unease to lead such a challenging company.
It is the best of cases. This presidency has sadly proven irrelevant.
Mr. Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in the coming months we will fully engage in the debate on the new CAP.

It is important to reach a good agreement, so I hope the Belgian presidency succeeds in achieving what the Spanish presidency did not manage to do, namely, steer the discussions in such a way that Member States commit to maintaining a policy in the future

a truly strong common agricultural policy, with sufficient funding to meet the needs of all 27 Member States, both new and old. And let it not happen again as in 2005, when funds were insufficient and modulations had to be invented.

In the upcoming reform of the CAP, we are betting on the viability of a sector that not only provides intangible goods and services, such as environmental stewardship, but should also ensure the supply of food to European citizens.

We have already witnessed painful situations of scarcity in the past, which is why it is necessary for the European Parliament to ensure security and supply based on community production as the primary objective of the upcoming reform, without neglecting other contributions such as the fight against climate change.

Mr. President, Commissioner, colleagues, I agree with Commissioner Cholos that this report is necessary, indeed very necessary, and arrives at an opportune moment, as the abuses committed by large-scale distribution have been demonstrated, severely disrupting the various links in the food chain.

The Bové report, which at times may go too far in attempting to be overly interventionist, must nevertheless be acknowledged—viewed through the lens of the social market economy that we at the European People's Party advocate—for addressing legitimate demands made by European producers.

Moreover, it brings transparency to the various parts of the food chain, which is always beneficial, and provides significantly greater opportunities and support for the weakest sectors within that chain, while also clearly supporting European consumers in gaining better knowledge of the chain.

The European food industry could have been given greater consideration in this report, perhaps, and that would have been very beneficial. However, this report also includes measures to curb price volatility, which was greatly necessary.

Measures are also being taken to ensure fairness in those links of the chain, as requested by the Environmental Commission. Efforts are also being called for to combat abusive and unfair practices by large-scale distribution.
It is high time to establish a list of abusive practices and for European legislation to prohibit them outright.
It is also important to implement measures to prevent difficult practices through private labels that undermine competition and violate intellectual property laws.

For all these reasons, we must support the BOVE report and all the colleagues from the different parliamentary committees—INCO, ENVIO, and Agriculture—who have worked diligently to ensure this report is finally published and can help European producers move forward.
The alarm bell sounds, and it is true
And frustration is also audible, as is evident in the statements made during this plenary session, regarding the failure to achieve the Millennium Development Goals.
And the truth is that what we are risking is considerable.

Our natural environment is so fragile that if one day the big fish decided it would no longer consume the small, we would all have to pay for it personally, generation after generation, using our health and well-being as the currency of exchange.
The European Union is today a leader in the fight against climate change and has been for some time, pioneering measures and conservation efforts.

Yet, as demonstrated by the unmet objectives, it appears that willpower was lacking—not only in implementation and coordination, which have also been mentioned here, but also in expansion, to go beyond our extremely limited special conservation areas.
Certainly, biodiversity is not a museum piece that can be confined to a ghettoized area, as sometimes seems to be the case, merely glanced at now and then.
Biodiversity lives and knows no borders. It spans the entire planet in a domino effect, where if one piece falls, all the others fall as well.

And I draw attention to the need for a more global and communal will, particularly regarding land, because subsidiarity as the sole method for managing land or forests may become one of the new formidable enemies of biodiversity.

That is why I draw attention to paragraph 67 and the possible vote that may follow, and I ask you to reject the second part, as I am convinced that a common policy on land would contribute to the conservation of the entire territory.

so varied, so diverse for better and for worse, and so lacking in political will among the states, yet so increasingly in need of European unity in the full sense of the term, and also greater unity for the land itself.

Thank you, Mr. President and Commissioner. First of all, I would like to highlight the work of the rapporteur, Mrs. Macablan, not only in substance but also in form, as she has carried out her task in collaboration with all the shadow rapporteurs.
I would also like to thank the experts from the Spanish Administration for their contribution to this agreement.

The objective of this European Union pharmacovigilance system is to strengthen the monitoring, transparency, and communication of the safety of marketed medicines, particularly with regard to their risk profiles and any adverse reactions they may cause.

It should be emphasized that the establishment of the European Pharmacovigilance Committee has been created, which will assess and issue recommendations to the European Medicines Agency regarding safety issues that may arise with medicinal products marketed in the European Union.

It is very important that the solution reached regarding the reporting of suspected adverse reactions to pharmacovigilance, both by Member States and by marketing authorization holders, has been established.

...Eudra Vigilance will be the sole source of information... but at the same time, the competent authority... of the Member State where the adverse reaction is suspected... to have occurred will also be informed... and this is very important...
It is also worth highlighting the opportunity afforded to consumers to report any adverse reaction, and in this regard, the establishment and management by the agency of an ecological portal.
The European portal on medicine safety, which in turn links to the portals of each Member State, will help improve citizens' understanding of the safety of the medicines they consume.
And finally, we cannot overlook the fact that, since pharmacovigilance plays a crucial role in protecting public health, the adoption of the directive and the
This directive and regulation will significantly contribute to this goal by improving not only current procedures but also the coordination of actions among the various Member States.
Thank you, Mr. President.

On this occasion, we will strive to achieve a more balanced position than the one we have today, one that takes into account both the rights of Internet users and the essential respect for intellectual property.

As a member of the Human Rights Commission, as a Spanish and European citizen, and as a victim of terrorism since the terrorist organization ETA murdered my brother and his wife in Seville, I address this Parliament to denounce the Government of Venezuela and its president for having allowed ETA terrorists to disappear.

...train in Venezuelan sleep... under the command of the alleged terrorist Cubilla... who works for the government of Hugo Chávez... despite the fact that an international arrest warrant is outstanding against him... constituting a serious violation... of the fundamental right to security... of any European citizen...
I demand that the Government of Spain take decisive action in response to this serious humiliation of all Spaniards.

Zapatero cannot remain silent while the Venezuelan ambassador labels our civil guards as torturers, despite the fact that they have been and will continue to be a model of bravery in the fight against ETA.
nor can it turn a blind eye to the growing cooperation between ETA and the FARC on Venezuelan soil, as Judge Eloy Velasco has already denounced.
Zapatero cannot shake hands with a leader like Chávez, who is turning Venezuela into a refuge for terrorists.

The president of a country like Spain, where terrorism has caused so much suffering, cannot continue to ignore this issue and should provide a response—worthy of the situation and the one any ordinary citizen would offer if given the chance.
On February 14, 2008, as a Valentine's Day gift, the Conference of Presidents decided that intergroups would meet only on Thursdays.
Since then, we have attempted to resolve this issue.
We have not achieved it.
Today, Mr. President, the committees have to meet on a date that is not appropriate...
They never have rooms and they never have interpretation.

Today I speak not only as President of the Intergroup on Poverty and Human Rights, but also with the mandate of the majority of the intergroups in this House, and therefore on behalf of the members of this House who are part of those intergroups.
And I solemnly request that the Conference of Presidents reconsider and revoke the decision of 14 February 2008 and restore the previous situation of freedom for the intergroups.
And if it does not, I politely say that we will bring the matter before the full assembly of this house, and it will be decided who in this house is sovereign—that is, this Parliament.
Mr. President, the International Day against Trafficking and Trade in Persons for the Purpose of Social Exploitation highlighted figures that must compel us to act promptly.
Over 90% of the prostitution carried out in Europe stems from blackmail and extortion.

And given these figures, we should ask ourselves whether we are doing everything necessary to protect the dignity and integrity of thousands of women whose rights are also safeguarded by the Fundamental Charter adopted through the Treaty of Lisbon.
Because we are not talking about the world's oldest profession, but about the only form of slavery that we have failed to eradicate from old Europe.
Countries such as Spain are making significant efforts in this regard, pursuing traffickers, raising public awareness, and promoting rehabilitation programs for female victims.
But it is not enough.
Europe must become a single space of action and commitment.

Mr. President, the regulation we are debating today is of vital importance for the future of an industry—the cutlery and related sectors—which in my region, the land of Don Quixote, specifically in Albacete, represents not only the survival of more than 8,000 jobs but also an element of identity and a forward-looking commitment to craftsmanship as a source of employment.

The Meidín sealing on the knives has long been claimed by social collectives such as Aprecu, and fiercely contested by the Government of Castilla-La Mancha with the unanimous support of its regional Parliament.
A border seal that is not interventionist is a commitment to the transparency of commercial rules and the fight against unfair competition.

It is to enhance consumers' decision-making capacity when choosing a product, so that they take into account not only the final price, but also the quality brand of the place of origin or the social conditions under which the product is produced.

And above all, it will represent a new horizon of hope for many people who are going through difficult times, yet who today look to us thinking that we have not remained indifferent in our commitment to a fairer trade—yes, but also in our effort to add value to those who, in addition to producing, do so with respect for social rights and under the best working conditions for European workers.
That is why I thank the opponent and Mr. Menéndez del Valle for their political support and social awareness on this important issue for my homeland.

prevent market turbulence from dragging us down and causing us to repeat the mistakes made throughout the 20th century. The euro, in third place, must serve as a herald of what Europe should represent in the world of democratic values and human freedoms.

Thank you, Mr. President. Madam Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I believe we are in a position to congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs. Meisner.

We accept the majority's position in favor of a legal situation with uniform rules for the entire Union; however, I shall not spare Your Lordships the clear observation that it also entails a certain lack of fairness.

Without a determined liberalization of the ports, they will not be able to expand the central role they must play within the context of intermodality.

Thank you, Mr. President. Integrated maritime policy is a cross-cutting, regional, and sectoral policy, which must include fisheries policy and agriculture, and this should not be subordinated to other policies.
We all must address environmental and economic challenges, both for society at large and for the fishing sector. New technologies, fishing gear, species development, a marine economy plan.

for greater employment, quality supplies, international cooperation that prevents piracy, and improved governance of our oceans, which know no boundaries, and to avoid sustainability issues such as those experienced in Iceland.
