Recently, the Church of Our Lady of Help in Baghdad was attacked, and 58 people were murdered, most of them women and children, solely because they were Christians.
Al-Qaeda has proudly claimed responsibility for the feat.
It is not an isolated incident, but the culmination of persecutions and attacks against Christians in countries with a Muslim majority.
Expulsions in Morocco, threats in Turkey, attacks in India or Nigeria.
In January, six Christians were massacred at the entrance of a church in Cairo.
Cairo. How long will the complicit silence of Western democracies last?
Fortunately, President Buseca has made an institutional statement.
The condemnation statement by High Representative Ms. Ashton has been limited to a condemnation of a terrorist act.
Why is the mobile phone not cited? The condition of being Christians, which is the unequivocal cause of the massacre.
I am hearing nothing from Mr. Zapatero. I have heard nothing from President Erdogan.
What is the Alliance of Civilizations for?
I feel shame for our foolishness and our cowardice.

Inevitably, Oriana Fallaci comes to mind—the weeping and misunderstood journalist who foretold the arrival of Eurabia and was nearly stoned for having the courage to expose what is happening in Europe today.
Thank you as well to the Lady Commissioner. I share the wish of this Chamber.
I want the Europeans' proposals to receive the support they deserve in Cancún.
Regarding this debate, the importance of cities in preserving the environment must be emphasized.

There is a pact among more than a thousand mayors from the European Union, demonstrating that these authorities recognize the existence of the problem, and their actions will be decisive if we are to succeed.
Great words mean nothing if we do not have the cooperation of the mayors of the cities.
To this end, it is very important that emissions be widely disclosed, as cities produce nearly 80% of greenhouse gases.
Today, there is no such dissemination, nor, worse still, a common standard for measuring these emissions themselves.
There is a non-profit organization called the CO2 Outreach Project, which already hosts information from thousands of companies and which I believe could also be used by municipalities.
Without their cooperation, without the collaboration of these municipalities and mayors, environmental action remains mere smoke—the very smoke we seek to escape.

Thank you, Mr. President. Some have argued that the condemnation of Morocco is too lenient, given the scale of human rights repression in the Sahara, where Morocco is nothing more than an occupying country with no sovereignty or legal title whatsoever.
But what matters most is the message that the entire Europe and this Parliament can no longer tolerate your violence.
Yesterday I was speaking about the Hague Tribunal in this very Parliament.

The international community, which sadly failed to prevent the genocide in Rwanda, Yugoslavia, and Darfur, I hope will nevertheless avoid another tragedy in Western Sahara, and that we will not have to see it appear before another tribunal, also in The Hague, as he said, but this time for war crimes.
Mr. President, Commissioner, the directive adopted in 2009 was a success because it updates the regulatory framework governing toy safety.
It is a significant step in the right direction because it indicates the path to follow, but it is not enough.
We must always remain vigilant and attentive to ensure that toys marketed do not endanger children's safety and health.
All toys available on the market must comply with current legislation.
We must review and improve surveillance mechanisms to detect hazardous toys, whether due to toxic substances, CMR substances, fragrances, or other hazardous substances such as HPA compounds.
It is necessary that the guidelines to be followed be clearer regarding the minimum age warning and the dangers associated with improper use.
It is necessary to ensure that the recommended age for toys is based on studies of children's behavior.
We must be able to adapt our regulations to scientific advancements supported by robust studies, and become more rigorous in monitoring the market and the importation of toys.
It is necessary to work together and strengthen cooperation among Member States, as well as ensure joint efforts by customs authorities to monitor the route of imported toys.
Citizens are preparing to make their holiday purchases, and we must give them the assurance that the products they buy meet all safety requirements.
In this debate, it has become clear that we all agree on supporting the objective of this report.
Different political groups have shown their support for this directive, which provides greater protection for victims.
Therefore, I wonder, what obstacle exists in approving this instrument?
I repeat, what fears are there that something as necessary and straightforward as ensuring equal protection for victims throughout Europe will ultimately become a reality?
Expecting that this Parliament will overwhelmingly approve this report, I address the Council and the Commission, reminding them that we have
overcome the legal obstacles in our negotiations. And I commend the Commission for acknowledging our efforts, just as we acknowledge the work it will undertake in the future.

We therefore need not exclude ourselves, but rather support one another by approving this more concrete instrument today, and tomorrow, the broader package of measures on victim assistance that the Commission will present, to which I commit myself to contributing.
Therefore, let us be courageous and responsible today, and help all those who have the right to live in a safer Europe.
Let us help everyone, I repeat, whether men, women, or children.
Let us help those who today look to us, so that we may give them a better life, a life that restores their freedom, their security, and above all, their dignity.

Mr. President, the European People's Party group will enthusiastically vote in favor of this report, and my first congratulations go to the rapporteurs, the committee, and the Council for this agreement.
We coined, Your Lordships, the term "democratic deficit" in the 1980s.
With it, we wanted to convey that in the European Union, decisions were made solely by the Council of Ministers upon proposals from the Commission, and this Parliament was merely consulted.
The battle of this Parliament since the 1980s, through the Maastricht Treaty, the Treaty of Amsterdam, the Treaty of Nizza, the Constitutional Treaty, and the Treaty of Lisbon, has been...

bridge that democratic deficit. And I believe that with the Treaty of Lisbon, with the new powers granted to the European Parliament, and with the involvement of national parliaments, we have achieved that goal.

And today we conclude this with this citizen legislative initiative, which emerged from the work of the Convention through the constructive cooperation between national parliaments and the European Parliament.

And I would like to recall here the names of Jürgen Mayer, our German Social Democratic colleague, and Anand Lamassur, who were the ones who brought that initiative to the full convention and ensured its approval.
Therefore, I believe we have completed the circle, Madam Chairwoman.
I believe that today, when we vote on this initiative, we will have placed, on the table, the death certificate of the concept of democratic deficit.
It is now our turn, Your Lordships, to act responsibly. And today we also have the opportunity to do so.
By voting on the Union's budget, we will fulfill what is expected of us: a responsible Parliament and a co-decision-making Parliament.
I believe it is a good day for Europe, ladies and gentlemen.
In January 2012, around 400 Spanish egg producers could disappear, approximately 30% of the total number in my country. With the loss of a production of 300,000 tons of eggs

And the European Union could cease producing those 80 million eggs, those 2 million tons, and we would merely end up having them replaced by imports from third countries, whose animal welfare standards are significantly lower than those of the European Union, if we do not act with great intelligence.
This 1999 directive requiring an increase in the space provided in cages for laying hens obliges us to act with intelligence and a proactive mindset, because otherwise...

We will only succeed in weakening European production and creating additional, commercial opportunities for third countries, where space per bird is infinitely worse than the current conditions we have in the European Union.

It is a directive that requires tremendous effort from European producers. In Spain alone, we can estimate the cost increase at around 600 million euros. However, we must not forget that it also has an economic impact extending to the egg-product industry and the food industry as a whole.

I therefore request the support of Your Lordships for the Popular Group's amendment to the second paragraph, which calls for seeking a solution at least for responsible farms and responsible entrepreneurs who are currently transforming their operations and will be nearly completed by January 2012.

We must support them in transforming those facilities and allow them sufficient time to complete the process, thereby avoiding irreparable harm to farms and preventing an abrupt shortage in the community market, which would consequently lead to higher prices for consumers.
It is about complying with the directive, giving the egg and egg product industry an opportunity, while also respecting animal welfare rights and consumers' right to reasonable prices.

Mr. President, I believe it is only fair to thank the rapporteurs, especially Mrs. Grostet and Mrs. Dagmar Romberg from my own group, for the work they have done to take this regulation out of stagnation and make it effective, as their efforts have enabled the inclusion of the right to public health as a legal basis.

and to establish a new direction that enables us to lay the foundations ensuring citizens' security and quality they choose at every moment. Especially because it also allows for clear regulation of the role of States, both those of origin that must make the payment and those receiving the treatment.

But I believe we should not be satisfied merely with this; rather, this must mark the beginning of a much greater advancement that, as Mrs. Romberen stated, transforms this directive into a mere formality, such that the level of equality achieved across all member states renders it unnecessary for citizens to move from one country to another.

And in this struggle, we must achieve a standard that better guarantees citizens' rights regarding the care they must receive, benefits, equivalence, and security, rather than merely the right they already have to mobility between Member States.
Equality is what will create unity, and without it, we will be taking very small steps.

Madam President, the Spanish Socialist delegation has voted in favor, as has the rest of our group; however, I would not feel at ease leaving here without expressing my concern that these provisions have been included in this directive...
the issue of organ transplants contrary to the position held by the Environment Committee, resulting from the Council's persistent insistence on including transplants in this directive.

I hope that this inclusion does not diminish the effectiveness of the good legislation we adopted in this same Plenary session, both the Transplant Directive and the Action Plan, very recently and with nearly unanimous support.

And I emphasize that we will remain vigilant to ensure that this inclusion does not undermine the excellent work carried out by organizations such as Spain's National Transplant Organization, and certainly not the goal of saving 20,000 lives that we had both in the transplantation directive and in the Action Plan, which I repeat was approved here.
Therefore, I make this clear and state that we will continue to monitor compliance with this directive in this regard.
Mr. President, today also marks the European Day against Sexual Health.
Ladies and gentlemen, the figures are concerning. The World Health Organization estimates that over 300 million people contract a sexually transmitted infection each year involuntarily.

In Europe, as the European Forum on Sexual Health warns, it is concerning that the number of unwanted pregnancies among adolescents is rising due to an excessive lack of education and information among our young people.

Indeed, as the Commission points out, there are various and diverse inequalities across Europe in access to sexual health services, depending on the country, and within these countries, depending on citizens' income levels.
To combat all of this, a European strategy is required.
Only in five member states are such plans in place, but in all of them, the number of unwanted pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, and abortions has been reduced.
A common strategy that emphasizes prevention through the educational sector, expands information policies, increases efforts in family planning, and enhances access to contraceptive methods.
A comprehensive and ambitious strategy, free from taboos, to ensure every citizen's right to sexual health under the safest and most hygienic conditions.
At a time of intense social tensions, one cannot but enthusiastically welcome the major social consensus agreement in Spain between the government, trade unions, and employers' associations.
This agreement is exemplary for Europe, where for some time now such milestones have not been achieved.

Also worth noting are the arguments put forward by Cándido Méndez, one of the main figures behind that Spanish pact, who attended the Economic and Social Council meeting in Brussels on Tuesday, February 8.
Cándido then emphasized the need to extend coordination to a European scale and the importance of having the voice and initiatives of trade unions within our Europe.
That is how it must be...

an attack or a massive influx of immigrants, such as the one currently taking place in Lampedusa, where the exodus of Tunisian citizens toward the Italian coast could lead to a chronicle of a death foretold.

Madam President, it has already been said: the backbone of pension systems in Europe is and must remain the public system, because it is the only system that ensures cohesion and also reduces the risk of poverty in our society.

It remains striking that in OECD countries, the highest levels of income equity among citizens are found in the area of pensions, or that due to the crisis, private pensions lost 20% of their value in 2008 alone, and still, to this day, remain far from the solvency levels required.

All of this demonstrates that, at a time of unavoidable reforms, the sustainability of our pension system must be addressed in a cross-cutting manner, taking into account, among other factors, ambitious policies on fertility or work-life balance.

In my opinion, this is essential for an entire new generation of workers who, if being required to do more in order to secure a decent pension, must likewise be granted easier access to decent employment.

Mr. President, Commissioner, we are at a pivotal moment in shaping the future cohesion policy, which, following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, addresses not only economic and social aspects but also territorial dimensions.
Territorial cohesion aims at achieving harmonious development among territories, enabling their citizens to fully capitalize on their respective territorial specificities.
However, to achieve this objective, it is also necessary to advance cooperation among the regions of the Union facing the same challenges and problems.

This is the objective pursued by the European Union's Strategy for the Atlantic Region: a shared perspective to address the common challenges faced by the countries in the region, including marine research, maritime surveillance, and environmental and economic challenges.

To achieve our objectives, it is necessary to adopt an integrated strategic approach aligned with the 2020 Strategy and the European Union's policies for the period following 2013, particularly regional and maritime policies.
However, ladies and gentlemen, above all it is necessary that this strategy be based on renewed multi-level governance, founded on closer involvement of public authorities
regional and local levels, Member States, the European Union, and stakeholders from the private sector and civil society organizations, including interregional organizations and networks.
It is a highly significant day to discuss equality between men and women, as we celebrate Women's Day, honoring those who a century ago fought for labor and economic rights and for women's suffrage.
We have made great progress over these 100 years, but much remains to be done, and true equality is still a dream, while the discrimination against women is a real fact.

I believe each of us, men and women alike, can do much more to achieve true women's emancipation. Because there are many women in the world whose right to live is threatened, whose dignity is violated, and whose freedom is denied.
Allow me to express, on this International Women's Day, my support and hope for all the women in the revolution in North Africa who are fighting for their democratic rights.
Because a woman cannot be worth half a man, neither in Arab countries nor anywhere in the world.
Therefore, we are of equal worth and deserve equal treatment.
And I will continue fighting to achieve it. And as long as that equality does not exist, the Women's Day will continue to exist.
Although there are many who do not wish to see it, it is a reality that there are women who earn half as much as men, and that domestic violence primarily affects women and children.
And I repeat, we must continue denouncing all such incidents in this Parliament, and certainly I pledge that I will continue working on this.

Thank you, Mr. President. Madam Commissioner, allow me first of all to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr. Swab, and Mrs. Gerhard on the excellent work they have accomplished.

Because if we wish to establish and pursue a genuine internal market for business-to-consumer relationships, we must establish

Mr. President, many experts are telling us that the next crisis could be an energy crisis, and that either we are not doing the necessary work to prevent it, or we are not doing so at the speed required to avoid it.
This coming Saturday, the World Forum for Nature calls on everyone to make a small gesture in the pursuit of sustainability.
Many citizens will turn off the lights for one hour, and many public administrations will follow suit.
But it is not enough.
Europe cannot continue relying on energies that are not its own, that are located far away, or on insecure energy sources.
We must help states build a new energy model in which renewable energies are the heart of the new system.
Moreover, we must promote awareness of new consumption patterns, because renewable energy and efficiency are undoubtedly the keys to future sustainability.

Finally, I would like to appeal to all countries of the Union to join this call, this commitment of five of them, including Spain, to increase CO2 emission reductions to 30%, thereby demonstrating that Europe, in addition to being a pioneer in freedom and rights, is also a leader in its commitment to planetary sustainability.
Thank you very much. The majority of times I have spoken in this Parliament, I have done so to denounce violence against women, and once again I am here to combat it.

I must say that not only do I not grow weary of it, but I consider it essential to continue speaking publicly about this violation of human rights, as no cause can be effectively championed without a platform, and this Parliament is the most suitable one.

We must be doing something wrong, and therefore it is time to abandon demagogy and move from words to actions by providing real assistance and the necessary attention so that women can live with dignity and security.

because the approval of these reports not only advances us toward the reactivation of the single market, a key element in confronting the current economic crisis, but also responds to citizens' demands for further European integration and the strengthening of social rights.

Mr. President, the blue tongue disease has been a constant threat to our livestock and, with it, to our public health.

A disease against which we were fighting through the use of an activated vaccine, whose application requires the establishment of restricted zones for animal movement, as the use of these vaccines may lead to unintended virus spread in non-infected animals.

For these reasons, the use of the vaccine was limited, restricting vaccination to those areas where infected animals had previously been found, thereby creating a double disadvantage for livestock farmers: on one hand, the disease itself, and on the other, the associated restrictions.

However, all these problems appear to be avoidable through the new inactivated vaccine, which not only prevents disruptions that might occur in the domestic livestock market but also enables the development of broader and more effective prevention strategies—what is truly essential in the fight against this disease.
For all these reasons, it is of vital importance for this sector that we amend the directive to allow its use, and that we do so as soon as possible.

Ladies and Gentlemen, compliance with the provisions of our treaties, particularly those concerning the correspondence tables, cannot serve as an excuse for this regulation not coming into force before summer, as our delay, and your delay, would only harm the livestock farmers.

First of all, I would like to thank the Petitions Committee for the work it has carried out following the complaint submitted by FEPEX, the Spanish Organization of Fruit and Vegetable Producers and Exporters, based on the report published in 2007 by OLAF, the European Union's Anti-Fraud Agency.

I believe the conclusions of that report are extremely clear and fully justify the fact that the European Commission should have already taken action long ago to clarify the functioning of the entry pricing system, at least in the tomato sector.

However, the Commission has not only failed to intervene to address potential loopholes created by the current entry price regulation, but has also negotiated a new agricultural agreement with Morocco without having previously resolved the shortcomings of the existing import regime.

Mr. President, it is high time that the European Commission took its responsibilities seriously and began investigating where frauds occur, and eventually demanded the recovery of unpaid customs duties.
I believe that a new agreement with Morocco must in no way be ratified without the details we have outlined having been resolved.

Our objective is not to halt the signing of international agreements, but to ensure that such agreements do not seriously harm the interests of European productions and that the rules established in these agreements are respected.

With regard to the new agreement with Morocco, the European fruit and vegetables sector is seeking only guarantees that the agreed measures will be effectively implemented. And this is possible only through the reform of the existing entry price regime.
Until we have a new entry pricing system, we cannot have a new agreement with Morocco.
We will not be able to ratify that agreement, which the committee has already finalized.

Moreover, if it is the responsibility of the Member States to carry out this control, then a significant economic effort would be required from them. But it is also true that we have long been calling for a European customs border system, similar to the one the United States has, in order to ensure effective control.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to begin my intervention by congratulating everyone who has made it possible to reach an agreement.

Mr. President, three remarks regarding the situation surrounding the cucumbers.
I am the grandson of a German and hold great affection for that country, but it is evident that the German authorities have acted in this case with haste and imprudence.

On the other hand, if we analyze the European response, we observe that the actions taken by the Food Safety Authority and the Commission have been muted, very weak, and imprecise. They have failed to coordinate or provide leadership.
Third, the affected parties have the right to receive economic compensation promptly.
But for the moment, I propose to this Parliament that we organize in Brussels or here in Strasbourg a major exhibition of European cuisine, precisely based on the cucumber.
The lost honor must be reclaimed—an expression pleasing to the German writer Heinrich Bell. The lost honor of cucumber producers must be reclaimed.
Europe is the appropriate setting for this.

The involved Member States have demonstrated slowness, inefficacy, and ineffectiveness, and to this day, even after 23 deaths, we still do not know where the problem originates.

The European Commission must seriously reflect on the problems caused by its irresponsible actions or inaction during crises such as the current one, resulting in cases of economic ruin and the destruction of jobs that were previously essential.

those 2.5 billion euros to repair, as much as possible, the damage caused.

To this end, it is necessary to find a legal framework that allows it. A legal framework that has already been found on other occasions, such as with mad cows or dioxins, and that should be found now.
Therefore, Commissioners, Ministers, today in Luxembourg you must rise to the occasion and meet your responsibilities.

German authorities, moreover, given the irresponsible allegations made, should undoubtedly and from the outset support this economic solution. They should also finance, using their own funds, communication campaigns to restore consumer confidence lost in horticultural and fruit production. Thank you.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, nothing we do here or in the European Union will bring back the dead, and I sincerely hope that what is being done will prevent further loss of life and break the chain of those who are suffering without having done anything to deserve it.

We do not know the causes, surely, but we do know certain things that we must name as they are. There has been negligence in political actions, and there has been negligence in the German federal government. It cannot act as if Hamburg were a city-state on another continent.

The arrogance that Mrs. Merkel and her Health Minister have shown in frequently addressing other states, if translated into diligent and effective coordination, would have prevented many of the events that have occurred.

And that negligence has had criminal consequences for a state that has been the Spanish state and for the most important sector of agriculture. It has led to the ruin of thousands of families and small entrepreneurs and has discredited the economy for many years.
I ask that you go to the Council and request full compensation for those affected, since we cannot ask for lives back for the dead.
and that control systems be reviewed.

Please review the controls, please review the alert systems, and demand accountability, because in Spain we are demanding it of you, Commissioner, of the Council, and of the German State.
Mr. President, we are facing a public health issue, an epidemic, as the Commissioner has stated, and in response to this, someone has been sought to blame, if not the actual cause.

The system has failed. We have 22 dead, 1,600 affected, and three weeks later we still do not know the cause. And appealing to a precautionary principle without scientific basis—again, without scientific basis—we blame Spain.
Honorable deputies, the Spanish cucumbers were not contaminated.
They were not contaminated. I repeat this because some contradictory versions have been heard here.
But to show you the significance of what these words have provoked, 300,000 jobs could be affected, a loss of confidence.
An epidemic knows no borders.

I agree with you, Commissioner. It knows no borders. We are demanding accountability for what has not been done, for what we must do immediately. Because Germany currently faces a risk situation. It has a higher number of affected individuals, but Spain is not to blame.
We are requesting compensation.
And what unites us here today are the victims, because no one should feel as unprotected as European consumers feel today.
And it is out of respect for the victims that we need clarity and accountability.

And it is my responsibility to report to the German authorities who unjustly and hastily accused Spanish cucumbers, and specifically the cooperatives from Málaga and Almería, which were models of agricultural operations and will not recover from these accusations.
Thousands of unemployed families are asking why. They need an answer, and above all, they need to know what their future holds. Not just in Andalusia, but throughout agricultural Europe.

Therefore, and trusting that experts will quickly find a solution, I demand that the Commission, when it speaks with the Council today, request immediate economic and moral measures for all producers—from Andalusia and from throughout Europe—who are looking to us with indignation but also with hope.
Instead of promoting research and European collaboration, German authorities have slandered and harmed cucumber producers and others.

Mr. President, it is no coincidence that Spain, Italy, Greece, Ireland, Portugal, and some other countries share our reservations about readily accepting the so-called Brovilleta call.

The coincidence, the criticism, stems from our condition as peripheral countries, which ultimately will be we who bear this peculiar tax, collected by the European Union states that hold geographic centrality.
The rationale underlying the eurovignette gives rise to major contradictions, and the supporting discourse grows increasingly fragile each day.

Although one may accept as a starting point the rationality of internalizing external costs, in the end it is untenable that to alleviate congestion only large-tonnage vehicles be penalized while passenger cars are left out, applying the old principle "the polluter pays" in a clearly discriminatory manner.

On another note, Your Lordships, the eurovignette, which was conceived as a source of funding for infrastructure, will ultimately allocate only 15% to the financing of transnational networks, and that not obligatorily.

but as a mere recommendation. In such a way that the incumbent Minister of Finance shall allocate the collected funds to the purpose deemed most suitable for the country's interests within the broad scope of infrastructure.
The final outcome will be yet another setback for freight transport by road, which today is the only means currently enabling the internal market.
This is the reality, ladies and gentlemen, a reality that is far harsher in the context of an economic crisis.

In this regard, the competitiveness of European products in global markets cannot afford experiments that might further deteriorate the already fragile state of the European economy through the imposition of additional costs.

The food crisis, which has recklessly been shifted onto Spanish products, has resulted in unjust penalties and massive losses for those it penalizes—the eurovignette, freight transporters, and farmers of premium crops.
These are the reasons, Madam Presidents and Members, why the Spanish delegation of the European People's Party will vote against this draft directive.

Well then, the objective of the report we have prepared is aimed at correcting energy infrastructures that, in the opinion of the European Commission, have become outdated and poorly interconnected.

Thank you, Madam President. From the European Parliament, I wish to pay tribute today to all women. Despite the significant progress we have made since March 1911, when women took to the streets demanding the right to vote and better working conditions, I believe that gender equality remains far from achieved.
Today women can work without having to ask permission from anyone, but in many cases they still earn less than men for similar work, which is unacceptable.
We must also remember the women in rural areas, whose work goes unrecognized.
I believe another uncomfortable reality, yet one that must continue to be denounced, is violence against women.

This year alone, an excessive number of women have already been murdered, not counting the serious threats made by those who believe they have the right to inflict physical or psychological violence upon them.
It is time to say enough, and to say it loudly and clearly.
From this Parliament, I reaffirm my commitment to the European Protection Order for Victims and to the Victims' Directive presented by the Commission, which will be voted on, God willing, next June.
We must also say, and I would like to emphasize, that motherhood continues to be an obstacle to women's advancement, and I believe this trend should be changed.
I want to send a message of hope, and with many women, with the growing support of men, we must advance along the path of equality, opportunities, rights, and also of responsibilities.
We have come a long way, but there is much still to be done.
I have to admit that I do not absolutely and deeply believe in quotas, but I have to tell you that something needs to be done, and I agree with
Many of my colleagues, and of course Ms. Reding, in opening this debate and advancing along this path so that women can regain the place they deserve—my support is available.

The European social model, which we so proudly uphold, is not based solely on our pension, education, or healthcare systems, but also on a particular approach to balancing the relationship between employer and employee within companies.

The labor reform approved by the Spanish Government, without dialogue or consensus, breaks with that social model and moves us away from the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, both in terms of job creation and in terms of job quality.
A labor reform by itself does not create jobs; economic growth does.
What labor law does do is define under what conditions workers enter companies, under what conditions they remain in them, and under what conditions they leave them.
And with this reform, where 80% of workers will be left unprotected because they will not be subject to collective agreements, where they could be dismissed due to a decline in company revenues,

They may unilaterally modify their schedules. What is clear is that workers will enter the labor market with greater precarity, remain in it with increased fear, and leave it with reduced compensation.

That is why this reform, which undermines the European social model and promotes the competitiveness of the Spanish economy solely in relation to workers' conditions, deserves a general strike such as the one that must be called tomorrow in Spain to defend this social model.

Madam President, since I entered this Parliament, I have not ceased to denounce female genital mutilation.
That silent and accepted crime endured by millions of women and girls around the world. Seventy million, I repeat, seventy million.

...an unbearable figure... for anyone who takes the time to examine... the terrible consequences... of these practices... hidden behind the guise... of psychological, health, religious... reasons—all of them false...

The truth is that the procedure, predominantly performed on girls aged 14 and even as young as one year old, can result in death due to hemorrhagic shock, and in the best-case scenario, cause severe psychological sequelae as a result of the trauma.
From Europe, we must fight with all our strength to eradicate this violation of human rights, which undermines the right to equal opportunities and endangers health.
Your Lordships, this is abuse, cruelty, and torture inflicted upon young girls. And this degrading treatment also exists in Europe.

When they ask me—when they ask me in my country what it is that I do in Europe—I will be able to say with great pride that I am legislating in favor of victims.
And that work personally honors me deeply and gives me great strength to continue working.

I am confident that with the support and commitment not only of those of us working here in Europe, but also of our colleagues in our respective countries, we will together—indeed, with Your Lordship's assistance—lift the victims out of that unjust oblivion.
Colleague Turmes has done an admirable job, because advancing a regulation of this nature required the courage of this rapporteur, who has skillfully overcome the many obstacles skillfully arranged.
It has been worthwhile because efficiency in energy use and its conservation are requirements of any energy policy worthy of the name.

We should commend significant measures such as the requirement for the renovation of public buildings, the imposition of a cumulative energy savings target on energy companies, as well as mandatory audits for large private enterprises.
Moreover, Turmes's and the other rapporteurs' efforts will be of no avail unless accompanied by decisive action from the Member States.
An action that in turn must be accompanied by a firm commitment to these objectives from the regions, municipalities, and European citizens.
I am interested in highlighting the involvement of these latter individuals.
At present, what is evident is a great deal of frivolity among authorities and among citizens.
Don't we, dear colleagues, see people seated on heated outdoor terraces during Europe's harsh winter?

Thank you, Mr. President. The trade agreement with Central America, Colombia, and Peru is an important one, and the safeguard clause aims—especially with the amendments we have proposed—to ensure it is straightforward, efficient, and provides adequate guarantees.

We have tabled amendments to protect European production in accordance with the treaties, and we are establishing security mechanisms for both European production and production in the countries where the agreement is implemented.
Protection of the outermost regions is necessary because they lack diversified agriculture, due to economic factors affecting a large number of
jobs, more than 10,000 direct jobs and 35,000 indirect jobs in Spain, in a population heavily affected by unemployment and by its specific characteristics
For example, the Canary Islands banana has significant specificity in agriculture, culture, and gastronomy.

But it is also necessary there, in the regions where we will establish the agreement—Central America, Colombia, and Peru—because trade is fundamental to development, and development entails labor rights, sustainability, and benefits for the producer.

Mr. President, the rapporteur's work is very good, but I wish to take this opportunity to say here, within this framework, that our prose is extremely unclear, as anyone who examines European legislation can readily observe.
We have forgotten what Pufendorf already taught, that general laws should be few in number and their meaning must be correctly understood.
Therefore, those who issue it must use as much clarity as possible.
And Montesquieu consistently maintained that legal language must be precise and concise.
In other words, we must avoid esoteric jargon, filled with neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, as it creates a malignant web where traps thrive and arbitrariness takes root.
We deal with very complex issues in this Parliament, but it is our duty to convey them to the citizenry in a comprehensible and accessible manner.
It is common for legal professionals themselves—judges, lawyers—to become frustrated when confronted with our texts.
Good prose, dear colleagues, is the legislator's courtesy and a way of demonstrating respect for the citizen.
Honorable deputy, I have just spent the time—however little I had—insulting and discrediting you from the most complete ignorance.
ignorance. You are familiar with the Spanish Constitution.

Because what the Vice President, Mr. Vidal Cuadras, has stated is nothing more or less than applying what is set forth in the Spanish Constitution. The rest are singular personal opinions that are entirely irrelevant and which I may respect, but which I disdain.

Thank you, Mr. President. Ladies and gentlemen, there exists an old principle that has moved from the legal realm into the political sphere: pacta sunt servanda. That is, agreements are meant to be fulfilled.
By this I mean the significant measures adopted by the European Council in June to address the crisis affecting us.

Since the adoption of these agreements, market pressures eased and these were positively received, as they may break the vicious cycle between banks and sovereign debt, as well as the plans to advance toward a genuine economic and monetary union and the commitment to defend the euro in an irreversible manner.
The European Council, held last week, contains, ladies and gentlemen, undeniable positive aspects. The conclusions regarding progress toward the Banking Union are satisfactory.

It is also positive that agreement A27 has been reached regarding the establishment of the banking supervisor. Finally, it is appropriate that the implementation be entrusted to the Eurozone's finance ministers.
But they will allow him to conduct a critical examination of the biased interpretations that have been unilaterally made of it.

Your Lordships, it is unacceptable to make particular interpretations that may distort the mandate granted to the Ministers of Economy, nor do we approve of matters being presented as agreements when they were never even discussed.
It is urgent to advance toward a single supervisory mechanism that must cover all financial institutions.
A common deposit guarantee scheme and a crisis resolution mechanism are also needed.
The survival of the euro is linked to the urgent creation of an effective banking union.
Thank you very much, Madam President. Ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, the advantages of the internal market are undeniable: increased competition, and a greater supply of products and services.
services, creation of new jobs, more affordable prices for consumers, and greater protection—still insufficient, and we cannot afford complacency.
However, cultural, linguistic, protectionist, and bureaucratic barriers prevent citizens and businesses from fully benefiting from the single market.
Half a year ago, we celebrated the launch of the 12 priorities aimed at stimulating growth and reinforcing citizens' confidence in the internal market.
Unfortunately, an agreement has not yet been reached on most of the proposals, as the Commission acknowledged in last week's communication.

Mr. President, Commissioner, the urgency of the crisis compels us to be ambitious, to go much further, and to reach agreements as soon as possible to promote growth, employment, and confidence among citizens and businesses in the single market.
Mr. President, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur and the other colleagues who have worked on this reform very adequately.

The financial crisis has intensified due to the existence of complex and obscure tradable assets linked to derivative instruments and transactions conducted outside official markets, all aimed at achieving high returns in a short period of time.
It is necessary to bring financial operations into a transparent market to ensure proper price formation and competitiveness of tradable products.
But above all, it is essential that the information provided be understandable to investors, who must have full awareness in order to freely consent to the risks they undertake.
Transparency is of no use as a brilliant flash of light if the confusion in the design of tradable products blinds citizens' understanding.
I like Mrs. Thyssen's report because I believe that anything which strengthens the role and authority of this Parliament in political debate is moving in the right direction.
Too often we hear accusations against European institutions regarding their limited democratic quality.
It is a statement made casually, and this is not the occasion to enter into this debate.

Yes, to emphasize that this Parliament possesses the same legitimacy as any national parliament, and it is even likely that, with regard to some, it operates under more transparent rules and additionally allows greater freedom to its members.
Therefore, all economic measures must pass through the filter represented by this Parliament before their final approval, and again when examining their implementation and results.

Mr. President, 5.3% of the active population works in the automotive sector.
The figures speak for themselves.
The decline in sales in the European market, combined with corporate strategy, leads, in general terms, to the outsourcing and restructuring of companies.
There is a crisis of overcapacity currently affecting 10 assembly plants and potentially 100,000 workers.

Unfortunately, there are few cases of restructuring with a long-term vision. Generally, a short-term perspective prevails, in which the main focus is gaining competitiveness through labor adjustments.

I must tell you that on the very same day that Ford communicated the shutdown of the Heng plant, the Valencia Ford company was informed that its production would be moved to Germany, in the same corporate announcement.

Therefore, we believe this is a general issue for Europe, and we consider it essential to establish a European legal framework that anticipates changes and underscores the importance of social dialogue.
Social dialogue is fundamental.
Mr. President, today we are debating four reports that represent a revitalization of our common policy on security and defense.

It is a clear indication of the conviction that our security and defense have a distinct European dimension, in contrast to the minority factions and Eurosceptic voices that also prevail in this Chamber.
The report by Mr. Danian, to whom I wish to express my gratitude for his spirit of consensus-building throughout its preparation, contains significant contributions from the Socialist Group.
among others, references to effective multilateralism, the responsibility to protect principle, or the concept of human security.
And we would like to see these aspects included in the possible review of the European Defence Strategy.
We have also pointed out that a strong common security and defense policy is of interest to our transatlantic alliance as well.
I must highlight another challenge that is acquiring significant dimensions for our global security.
It is the one concerning cybersecurity and cyberdefense, reports that we also discussed today and whose recommendations we hope will be included in the new European cybersecurity strategy.
In a context of severe economic and financial crisis, during which member states are being forced to cut their defense budgets, European cooperation projects are needed most.
That is why it is more important than ever to strengthen the role of the European Defence Agency, which is currently facing significant criticism.
I conclude by stating that we can relaunch European security and defense, and do so with President Ford Lapui's proposal for the December 2013 Council.
Thank you very much to the four speakers.
Mr. President. Ladies and gentlemen, once again the defense of the general interest, championed by the Commission and Parliament, confronts national self-interests that prevail in the Council.
Given the prior acceptance of the legitimacy of both positions, reaching a balance is inexcusable.
The relevance of the upcoming financial framework is unquestionable.
The Spanish delegation of the PPE and I, on its behalf, reaffirm that, in our view, the Presidency's proposal is unacceptable.
A cut of 75 billion euros from the already inevitably meager European Commission proposal proves sterile and useless.
And that is not all. The new direct cut to agricultural and cohesion policies goes beyond the red line that Spain had established from the outset of the negotiations.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is no longer the time to debate a supposed dichotomy between austerity and growth.
Some of us are making proven efforts and sacrifices to correct the deficit.
But Spain, like many other countries, needs to grow, increase economic activity, and create jobs.
And with this budget, one achieves nothing. On the contrary, it serves as a missile aimed at the financial line of defense.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is not possible to achieve more Europe with fewer resources, and especially not if what exists is distributed unjustly.

If that is the case, Spain will oppose a financial framework for the European Union that amounts to the outright elimination of growth prospects and, above all, any remaining trace of solidarity. And this is precisely the direction the European Council is taking.
Fortunately, this is not the path indicated by the Commission, to whom we must express our gratitude for its commitment to charting a different route.

At this point, in the need for unity, in the need for shared efforts, I wish to thank Mr. Barroso for his firm declaration, delivered the other day in Cádiz, interpreting the treaties unequivocally in the case of a secessionist scenario within the Union.

It made clear, unequivocally, as guardian of the treaties, that if a part of a member state separates as an independent state, it becomes regarded as a third country and, therefore, the treaty's provisions and effects will not apply to it in any way.

I hope, Mr. Vice President, in the absence of the President, that this may be taken up and that this safe and consistent doctrine of the Commission may continue to remain alive for the protection of all.
